item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
I have been grateful that several of my articles have been included in the WikiWalk project thus far, and I have approved all previous crosslinks. Unfortunately, I cannot approve the proposed crosslinks here.

- While I recognize the superficial similarities with 785, an explicit link implies a similar underlying cause for the phenomena. And while I acknowledge that the addenda for 2098 may be slow, they are intended to point towards a potential origin for the effects outlined. This origin is entirely dissimilar to any proposed through 785 and I believe it will not enrich either article to have them linked in this fashion.

- Along similar lines, while I respect the diverse work of many authors regarding the Fifthist concept, I do not believe either it nor 2098 are enriched by an explicit link between them.

I am not opposed to any crosslink for the article, but the ones proposed simply do not fit in my estimation. The article has (in what I consider to be a gracious compliment) already been linked to Kalinin's proposal with great effect, for instance.

I would counter-propose any link the WikiWalk wishes to investigate within the expanded articles/tales regarding the study of memetics and antimemetics. One of the intents with the article was to demonstrate the use of an amnestic to try and actually restore natural memories, so I would humbly suggest that may be more fertile ground.

My thanks for the consideration.

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: I think the latter link is too strong a connection, basically implying that this is Roman Fifthism, which I'd be surprised if that was the author's intent.

item.title: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Edit: proposal vetoed by author

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

This SCP is really similar to SCP-785. They're both virulent ideas about some cultural thing with a malevolent twist. In addition, the last addendum gives me vibes similar to that of SCP-1425, especially the "Procession of Shortest Waves" and the "Communion of the Wavelengths".

Editing this:

SCP-2098 will begin to manifest in a target community through altered signage/advertising for a local gathering.

To this:

SCP-2098 will begin to manifest in a target community through altered signage/advertising for a local gathering.

Editing this:

Event 2098-18: "Saint Simon's Communion of the Wavelengths"

To This:

Event 2098-18: "Saint Simon's Communion of the Wavelengths"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is one of my favorites. The idea is interesting, but the way this is executed takes the cake. There are a few slow spots, particularly in the addenda, though I'm willing to overlook this for the classic, old-school feel this emanates.

+1

P.S. neither "meme" nor "memetic" shows up once in this SCP, which makes it even better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wonder what would happen if a green vector was made to participate in multiple Saint Simon's days that occur on differing dates? Or a red vector for that matter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Do you become a red-vector if you celebrate on your birthday when you're already green? If so, this is way more dangerous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting idea, great execution. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm going to consider this praise from Caesar. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is fantastic! A great example of how to write a K-Class without resorting to "and then everybody dies and the world explodes." At first it seems relatively innocuous, and then you start to realize how incredibly big this is, how hard it could be to contain, and how massive a societal shift can come from something so silly as a fake holiday. I love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really, really like this one. It's a really unique idea, clearly carefully thought-out and well-executed, and has great little funny chunks scattered through in the names of the events. Reminds me a bit of the Fifth Church - which is great, because I always found them thoroughly interesting and creepy in equal measures.

On a side note, I'm looking forward to my local youth centre's Catharsis of Saint Simon's Resonant Corpus next Saturday!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A Skip doesn't have to have an active desire to destroy humanity to be K-Class. I argue for K-Class status simply because this changes the fundamental way humans function in society.

item.title: Scary!
item.content:encoded: The reason why is I hate holidays, especially Christmas. This reminds me of Christmas, so naturally I'd be terrified of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is fucking phenomenal imo. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Makes sense. Thanks for the explanation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think it implies that in the author's headcanon, "Prime Susceptibility Communities" is standard Foundation lingo for things like this. For example, there could be instances of PSC-3427 or what have you.

That's how I understand it, anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fixed "catechesis".

D'oh.

But I'm glad you enjoyed it. Thank you for your feedback.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I adore articles where the skip can be summed up as something really unique, and "virulent holiday" fits that bill and how. I cannot overstate my love for this skip. It's got a semi-classic feel (really more of an early series II, oddly), and you didn't go overboard with the Keter while still making a good case for the classification. The writing is solid, the idea is sound, and…

The Red-Vectors. Oh my god, a day becomes Saint Simon's Day because Simon says.

Also, it's "Catechesis". Also also, I love all the event names, but I think "Saint Simon's Day Bisected Youth Vigil" is my favorite. Bravo!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good work. A very unique concept for an article, thoroughly documented and well-considered in terms of the ramifications of the skip. The supplemental materials are also given enough context to fit nicely within the overall narrative.

Interestingly, the comments so far have questioned the Keter classification and whether or not this is actually dangerous. When I read this, one of my favorite features of the article was its recognition of how much societal disruption would result from the entire population celebrating this anomalous holiday. If everyone in the world celebrates this holiday, and enough people become Red vectors, you have a sizable contingent of humanity that now believes it's Saint Simon. Assuming that the displacement of culturally-significant events worldwide wasn't disruptive enough in unforeseen ways.

I also found the concept of intergenerational memory fascinating. It's a nice little add-on to the article, but I think it would be interesting to see something like that explored in a little more depth, either here or elsewhere.

Overall, solid job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a really good read. Although not dangerous, it still manages to be a palatable Keter.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love it. Just think of the holiday that never ends.

It's like that one Jimmy Neutron episode where every day is his birthday. Only nobody, even him, would notice or care.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If that's what it stands for, then why is there a -2098 following it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What are you guys talking about? There's no such place.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really enjoyed this. Have an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: PSC can stand for "Prime Susceptibility Communities" in the context of the article. Thank you for taking a look.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There was a "PSC" in the Containment Procedures, but other than that, this is damn good. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought it was a Republic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't help but think that there should be a St. Simon's Day pilgrimage to the Islamic Union of East Samothrace.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really fucking like this. This appeals greatly to my sensibilities. Also, I think the Keter classification is fully justified, given the amount of time, energy, manpower, and resources that have to be dedicated to containing this, combined with the fact that we have no idea what this thing is actually doing to people in the long term.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No problem, 's what I do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fixed the punctuation. Thank you.

As for the object class, I was trying to experiment with something that wouldn't destroy humanity, but rather fundamentally change it in a small way. The whole species is potentially compromised, so I went with Keter. We'll see how that pans out. Thanks for the feedback.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Cool.

My only nitpick: I don't think this is really a Keter. It hasn't shown an active desire to destroy human life, just edit it. In my mind, this is definitely a Euclid.

(Also, period missing at "vectors" end of the "red" segment.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am aware that posting this to the mainlist means summary judgment of finished work.

Enjoy.
